1	# Archon Project
2	
3	You are one of: the plan agent, a prover agent, a subagent (per descriptor in `.archon/subagents/`), or the review agent. Read `PROGRESS.md` to determine your role and current objectives. Keep workspace tidy. Prefer existing MCP tools.
4	
5	## Priority Rule
6	
7	When instructions conflict between global and local sources, **local takes precedence**:
8	
9	- Prompts in `.archon/prompts/` override Archon's global prompts.
10	- Skills in `.claude/skills/` override globally installed plugins.
11	- Rules in `.claude/rules/` apply only to this project.
12	
13	## Skills
14	
15	- **archon-lean4** — installed as `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source). Provides `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, etc.
16	
17	## Tools
18	
19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts. List with `ls .claude/tools/`; run `<path> --help`. MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json` and surface as `mcp__*` tools.
20	
21	Two always-present scripts:
22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs (OpenAI/Gemini/OpenRouter) for an informal proof-style sketch when you're stuck on an approach and want a second opinion. **Requires `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` in env** — check via `env | grep -E "OPENAI|GEMINI|OPENROUTER"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (one wrapper handles every subagent — see "Subagents" below).
25	
26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.
45	
46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. (The frontmatter field is still named `mandatory: [...]` for backward compat with existing configs and descriptors — its semantic meaning is "highly recommended", not "must dispatch".)
47	
48	## Key Files & Permissions
49	
50	All Archon state files are in `.archon/`. The table below covers **phase agents** (plan, prover, review) and the **user**. Subagent permissions are NOT a fixed row — they are descriptor-driven (`write_domain` glob in the subagent's frontmatter, enforced at dispatch time). See "Subagent permissions" after the table.
51	
52	| File | Plan | Prover | Review | User |
53	|------|------|--------|--------|------|
54	| `PROGRESS.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
55	| `STRATEGY.md` | read + write | — | — | read |
56	| `USER_HINTS.md` | **loop-managed** (captured + cleared by the CLI; injected into the plan prompt) | — | — | write |
57	| `task_pending.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
58	| `task_done.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
59	| `task_results/<file>.md` | read (collect) | write (own file) | read only | read |
60	| `proof-journal/` | read | — | **write** | read |
61	| `PROJECT_STATUS.md` | read | — | **write** (Knowledge Base only — session log lives in `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`) | read |
62	| `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` | **write** (this iter only) | — | read (recent window injected) | read |
63	| `iter/iter-NNN/review.md` | read (recent window injected) | — | **write** (this iter only) | read |
64	| `iter/iter-NNN/objectives.md` | optional write | — | read | read |
65	| `archon-protected.yaml` | read | read | read | **write** |
66	| `.lean` files | — | write (own file; frozen protected signatures) | — | write (via comments) |
67	| `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` | **write** (informal prose, `\lean{...}` hints) | — | **write** (semantic markers: `\mathlibok`, `% NOTE:`, `\lean{...}` corrections — NOT `\leanok`) | read |
68	| `analogies/<slug>.md` | read (when relevant) | — | — | read |
69	| `TO_USER.md` | — (review owns) | — | **write** (reset at review start) | read |
70	
71	### Subagent permissions
72	
73	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
74	
75	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).
76	- `read_only: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may only write its `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`.
77	- `can_spawn: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may dispatch children whose `write_domain` is a subset of its own.
78	
79	The dispatcher enforces `write_domain` at the filesystem level: any child whose declared domain is not a subset of the parent's is rejected before the child agent starts. Reads are restricted only by the descriptor's prompt body (e.g. fresh-context critics that are told not to read PROGRESS.md / STRATEGY.md).
80	
81	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md`** is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow run by hand by the mathematician. The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. To invoke a refactor-style subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic wrapper. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow — treat those as historical noise.
82	
83	## Protected Declarations
84	
85	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:
86	
87	- **Plan / prover / review** agents: read-only on protected signatures. May fill proof bodies; may not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
88	- **Write-capable structural subagents** (per their `write_domain`): may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (name + signature preserved verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.
89	- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.
90	
91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	## Agent Roles
112	
113	### Plan Agent
114	
115	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for full instructions.
116	- User hints, blueprint-doctor findings, recent iter sidecars, the subagent catalog, and the references summary are all pre-injected into your invocation prompt. You do not "go read" those files.
117	- Read `task_results/` to collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md`.
118	- Optionally invoke subagents (via the auto-injected catalog) before writing prover objectives. Subagent reports are auto-archived to `logs/iter-NNN/` by the Archon CLI.
119	- Write `PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round.
120	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch markers — `\leanok` is owned by the deterministic sync; `\mathlibok` / `% NOTE:` by the review agent.
121	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself.
122	
123	### Prover Agent
124	
125	- Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
126	- Read the stage-specific prompt from `.archon/prompts/` (`prover-autoformalize.md` / `prover-prover.md` / `prover-polish.md`).
127	- Write results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
128	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — never edit another agent's file.
129	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your file for file-specific hints.
130	- Do NOT edit blueprint chapters. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker; the review agent applies them.
131	
132	### Subagents
133	
134	Subagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime. The catalog is auto-injected into the plan and review prompts each phase (see "Subagents" above for the wrapper invocation). Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. Descriptors with `can_spawn: true` may dispatch children (subject to the global `loop.max_parallel` cap).
135	
136	### Review Agent
137	
138	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for full instructions.
139	- Read `proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data.
140	- Write the session journal to `proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (`summary.md`, `milestones.jsonl`, `recommendations.md`).
141	- Update `PROJECT_STATUS.md` (Knowledge Base only — session narrative goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`).
142	- Maintain the semantic blueprint markers — `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE:` annotations, stale `\notready` removal. Do NOT touch `\leanok`.
143	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify `PROGRESS.md`.
144	
145	### Loop infrastructure (no agent role)
146	
147	These run automatically between phases:
148	
149	- **Iter sidecar init** — creates `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/` at iter start so plan + review have a stable destination for per-iter narrative. Top-level files (STRATEGY.md, PROJECT_STATUS.md, task_*.md) stay bounded.
150	- **`sync_leanok`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically updating `\leanok` markers based on actual sorry counts and compilation status.
151	- **`blueprint-doctor`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically detecting orphan chapters, broken `\ref` / `\uses` / `\proves`, and new `axiom` declarations. Findings are written to `logs/iter-NNN/blueprint-doctor.{md,json}` and inlined into the next plan agent's prompt.

1	# Prover — Prover Stage
2	
3	You fill `sorry` placeholders with complete proofs in your assigned `.lean` file.
4	
5	## Workflow
6	
7	1. Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
8	2. Read `task_pending.md` for prior attempts, dead ends, relevant lemmas on your file.
9	3. Check the `.lean` file for `/- USER: ... -/` comments (file-specific user hints).
10	4. **Read the relevant blueprint chapter before writing Lean code.** The chapter holds the mathematical proof sketch you must align with. When stuck, re-reading the blueprint is often the fastest path forward.
11	5. Replace `sorry` with Lean proofs. Push as far as possible.
12	6. **Always save partial progress in the code.** If you can't fully close a sorry, leave your best attempt — commented-out steps, helper lemmas, partial `by` blocks with `sorry` at the stuck point. The file must still compile (use scoped `sorry` if needed), but your work must be visible for the next agent to continue from. NEVER revert to a bare `sorry` — that wastes the work.
13	7. Write your results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
14	
15	**Write permissions**: only your assigned `.lean` file(s) and `task_results/<your_file>.md`. Do NOT edit `PROGRESS.md`, `task_pending.md`, `task_done.md`, blueprint chapters, or other agents' files.
16	
17	## Protected declarations
18	
19	`archon-protected.yaml` lists declarations with **frozen signatures**. You may fill their proof bodies but must not rename, re-type, reorder arguments, or weaken hypotheses. Only the mathematician edits protected signatures.
20	
21	## Avoid early termination
22	
23	- Don't abandon a proof prematurely. Many complex proofs run to thousands of lines.
24	- Difficulty is NOT a valid reason to leave a `sorry`.
25	- Don't delegate to "the next iteration" or "another prover" if more effort could close it.
26	- Only modify the proof for your assigned task — leave unrelated proofs untouched.
27	- **Decompose**: break the proof into smaller sub-problems (following the blueprint's lemma structure when available: L1, L2, L3, …) and solve each individually until the entire goal closes.
28	
29	## Task completion criteria
30	
31	Your task is complete ONLY when ALL of:
32	
33	1. Every `sorry` in scope is replaced with a complete proof.
34	2. Zero axioms introduced.
35	3. The file compiles cleanly.
36	
37	## Never weaken the type to dodge the proof
38	
39	When the substantive type is unattainable this iter, you have one honest move: leave `sorry` with the **intended type signature** so the type itself encodes the claim. You must NOT substitute a tautologically-true type to trivialize the body. Three patterns are banned:
40	
41	- **Reflexive-iso placeholder.** Replacing a non-trivial `X ≅ Y` (or `Nonempty (X ≅ Y)`) target with `Nonempty (X ≅ X) := ⟨Iso.refl _⟩`. The type now asserts nothing; the docstring's "iter-N placeholder" disclosure does not undo the structural lie, and the blueprint's `\lean{...}` cross-reference will resolve to this hollow declaration.
42	- **`Classical.choice` body around an explicit witness.** Writing `Classical.choice (α := X) ⟨witness⟩` to dissolve `Type` vs `Prop` friction — the witness is unreachable through any unfold, so the def is indistinguishable from `Classical.choice ⟨any-other-X⟩` downstream. Either use `Classical.choose` on the existential chain (preserves the spec), or `Nonempty.some`-with-witness; or ship a typed `sorry`.
43	- **Empty-content `proof_wanted`.** `proof_wanted` discards the declaration post-elaboration — the named decl never enters the environment, so the blueprint's `\lean{...}` cross-reference breaks. Use `theorem name : T := by sorry` with the intended signature instead.
44	
45	**Litmus test**: if you `unfold` your declaration, does it expose the named substantive content (Kähler differential module, the explicit iso, …) or does it stop at `Classical.choice` / `Iso.refl _` / nothing? If the latter, the body is structurally vacuous — ship the typed `sorry` instead.
46	
47	## When infrastructure is missing
48	
49	Do NOT report "Mathlib lacks X" and stop. Before giving up:
50	
51	1. **Use the informal agent** (`.claude/tools/archon-informal-agent.py`): "Prove [goal] without using [missing infrastructure], only Mathlib." Even an imperfect sketch is valuable.
52	2. **Try the alternative** — formalize whatever the informal agent suggests.
53	3. **If you still can't**: write the alternative sketch to `informal/<theorem_name>.md` and record in your task result what you tried, why it failed, AND the alternative route you found. "I couldn't prove X, but here's approach Y that might work because Z" is far more useful than "infrastructure missing".
54	
55	When stuck more generally: break into smaller subgoals, search Mathlib more thoroughly, prove missing helpers yourself, try alternative strategies, re-read the blueprint, use Web Search for published proofs.
56	
57	**Impossibility vs difficulty**: technical difficulty → keep trying. Mathematical impossibility → immediately backtrack and document why.
58	
59	## Proof style
60	
61	- **Never modify working proofs** — if a declaration has no `sorry` and compiles, do not touch its body.
62	- Keep edits minimal; don't delete comments or change labels; don't add unrelated declarations.
63	- Helper lemmas you introduced may be modified if they turn out wrong.
64	- Add a concise comment above each helper lemma so reuse is easy.
65	- **`change` vs `show`** — `change` reshapes the goal up to defeq; `show` is purely display-level annotation. Using `show` where `change` is needed produces a linter warning. Default to `change` when in doubt.
66	
67	## Mathlib tags in PROGRESS.md
68	
69	The plan agent tags suggested lemmas:
70	
71	- `[verified]` — confirmed to exist.
72	- `[expected]` — guessed by naming convention. Quick `lean_local_search`; pivot if it doesn't exist.
73	- `[gap]` — verified NOT in Mathlib. Don't waste search time; formalize a workaround.
74	
75	## LSP MCP tools
76	
77	The `archon-lean-lsp` server exposes Lean LSP operations as **MCP tool calls** (full names like `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_goal` / `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_diagnostic_messages`). The lean4 skill reference uses short names (`lean_goal`, `lean_local_search`, …) — same tools, NOT shell binaries.
78	
79	- Always invoke through your tool-call interface, the same way as `Read` or `Grep`.
80	- **Never** call `Bash` with `lean_goal …` etc. — there is no such shell command.
81	- First LSP action: `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_diagnostic_messages` on your file. If it returns `success: false` the server is cold; retry once or run `lake build` once via Bash, then retry.
82	
83	## Search protocol
84	
85	Follow `references/lean-lsp-tools-api.md`. Key priority:
86	
87	1. `lean_local_search` first.
88	2. `lean_leansearch` for semantic search — describe the mathematical content, not just the name.
89	3. `lean_loogle` for simple type patterns only.
90	4. Never use shell `find` / `grep` to locate Mathlib theorems.
91	
92	## Tooling traps
93	
94	- **Trust `goals_after`, not just empty diagnostics**: in `lean_multi_attempt`, `diagnostics: []` can be misleading (line-scoping quirks attach errors to the enclosing `by` block). Check that `goals_after` is empty or has advanced.
95	- **Beware `lean_run_code` with imports**: standalone snippets can silently swallow elaboration errors when they contain imports. Rely on the actual file's `lean_diagnostic_messages` for authoritative verification.
96	
97	## Logging
98	
99	Write to `task_results/<your_file>.md` (mirror your `.lean` path: `Algebra/WLocal.lean` → `task_results/Algebra_WLocal.lean.md`):
100	
101	```markdown
102	# Algebra/WLocal.lean
103	
104	## wLocal_iff (line 45)
105	### Attempt 1
106	- **Approach:** Direct case split on maximal ideals
107	- **Result:** FAILED — needed IsLocalRing instance, not available
108	- **Dead end:** direct case split without IsLocalRing
109	
110	### Attempt 2
111	- **Approach:** Stacks 0A31, characterize via bijection on spectra
112	- **Result:** RESOLVED
113	- **Key insight:** `PrimeSpectrum.comap_injective` bridges the gap
114	
115	## helper_bijective (line 78)
116	### Attempt 1
117	- **Approach:** Split into injectivity + surjectivity; prove injectivity
118	- **Result:** PARTIAL (branch closed) — injectivity closed; surjectivity remains
119	- **Next step:** `PrimeSpectrum.range_comap_of_surjective` for the right branch
120	- **Lemmas found:** `PrimeSpectrum.comap_surjective`
121	```
122	
123	**Rules**: one section per theorem/lemma in your file. Each attempt records approach, result (RESOLVED / FAILED / PARTIAL / IN PROGRESS), dead-end warnings or next steps. Log negative search results ("Searched 'projective module infinite rank' — nothing in Mathlib"). The plan agent merges these into `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md` next iter.
124	
125	**Read-only context**: read `task_pending.md` (prior attempts on your file) and `task_done.md` (when the problem resembles a completed one). Do not write to either.
126	
127	## End-of-session handoff
128

9	% curves is the function-field extension degree; read from PDF page 316)

15	\section{Setup and motivation}
16	
17	This chapter is the fourth and final sub-build chapter
18	\texttt{RR.1}--\texttt{RR.4} closing the project's headline \emph{RR bridge}
19	\[
20	  \mathtt{genusZero\_curve\_iso\_P1}
21	  \,\colon\,
22	  \text{a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve $C / \bar k$ with
23	  $g(C) = 0$ is isomorphic to $\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$.}
24	\]
25	The chain assembled across the four sub-build chapters is:
26	\begin{itemize}
27	  \item \texttt{RR.1} (\Cref{chap:RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor}): the Weil-divisor
28	    group $\Div(C)$, the degree map
29	    $\deg \colon \Div(C) \to \mathbb Z$ (\Cref{def:divisor_degree}), and the
30	    degree-zero principal-divisor identity
31	    (\Cref{thm:principal_deg_zero}).
32	  \item \texttt{RR.2} (\Cref{chap:RiemannRoch_RRFormula}): the Riemann--Roch
33	    formula in genus zero $\ell(D) = \deg(D) + 1$ for $\deg(D) \geq 0$
34	    (\Cref{thm:riemannRoch_genus_zero}).
35	  \item \texttt{RR.3} (sibling chapter, dispatched parallel iter-174):
36	    the line bundle $\mathcal O_C([P])$, its global-sections identification
37	    $\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C([P])) = 2$, and the corollary
38	    \texttt{cor:nonconstant\_function\_genus\_zero} producing a non-constant
39	    rational function $f \in H^0(C, \mathcal O_C([P])) \setminus
40	    \bar k \cdot 1$ on a genus-$0$ curve with a marked $\bar k$-point.
41	  \item \texttt{RR.4} (\emph{this chapter}): the global classification ``a
42	    smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve $C / \bar k$ with $g(C) = 0$
43	    and a $\bar k$-rational point is isomorphic to $\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$.''
44	\end{itemize}
45	This chapter consumes \texttt{RR.3} (the non-constant function) and produces
46	the Lean target \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.genusZero\_curve\_iso\_P1} pinned at
47	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/AbelianVarietyRigidity.lean} line~290 and restated as
48	the interface block \Cref{prop:genusZero_curve_iso_P1} of
49	\texttt{AbelianVarietyRigidity.tex}. The \texttt{RR.4}-closure of that target
50	also drops the \texttt{sorryAx} propagation tagged on
51	\texttt{rigidity\_genus0\_curve\_to\_grpScheme} via
52	\Cref{thm:rigidity_genus0_curve_to_AV}.
53	
54	\paragraph{Scope of the $\bar k$-base classification.} The chapter restricts
55	to the case where the base field $\bar k$ is algebraically closed. Over $\bar
56	k$ every closed point is $\bar k$-rational, so the ``$C$ has a $\bar
57	k$-rational point'' hypothesis of Hartshorne's Exercise~IV.1.3 is automatic
58	(the smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve $C / \bar k$ is non-empty,
59	hence has closed points, all of which are $\bar k$-rational). The general
60	``$k \to \bar k$ descent'' step ``genus-$0$ curve over a non-closed $k$ with
61	a $k$-rational point $\cong \mathbb P^1_k$'' is a separate sub-build hosted
62	in \texttt{Jacobian.tex} (the \texttt{genusZeroWitness.key} downstream
63	consumer) and is out of scope here.
64	
65	\paragraph{Standing hypotheses.} Throughout this chapter, $\bar k$ is an
66	algebraically closed field and $C$ is a smooth proper geometrically
67	irreducible curve over $\bar k$. The Lean signature
68	(\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/RationalCurveIso.lean}) threads the
69	project's standing curve typeclass package:
70	\begin{quote}

72	   (C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))) [IsProper C.hom]\\
73	   \phantom{}\quad[SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom]\\
74	   \phantom{}\quad[GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom]}
75	\end{quote}
76	matching the typeclass set used by the headline target

82	(\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.genus}); the concrete projective line target

87	\section{The morphism to $\mathbb P^1$ from two global sections}
88	
89	\begin{lemma}
90	[Morphism to $\mathbb P^1$ from a globally generating pair of sections]
91	  \label{lem:morphism_to_p1_from_global_sections}
92	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.morphismToP1OfGlobalSections}
93	  \uses{def:codim1_cycles}
94	  Let $X$ be a scheme over a field $\bar k$, let $\mathcal L$ be an invertible
95	  sheaf on $X$, and let $s_0, s_1 \in H^0(X, \mathcal L)$ be two global
96	  sections whose common zero locus is empty:
97	  \[
98	    \{x \in X : s_0(x) = 0 \text{ and } s_1(x) = 0\} \;=\; \emptyset.
99	  \]
100	  (Equivalently, $s_0$ and $s_1$ generate $\mathcal L$ at every point.) Then
101	  there exists a unique morphism of $\bar k$-schemes
102	  \[
103	    \varphi_{(s_0, s_1)} \colon X \longrightarrow \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}
104	  \]
105	  whose pullback of $\mathcal O_{\mathbb P^1}(1)$ is $\mathcal L$ and whose
106	  pullbacks of the standard coordinate sections $x_0, x_1 \in
107	  H^0(\mathbb P^1, \mathcal O(1))$ are $s_0$ and $s_1$ respectively. On the
108	  basic-open chart $D_+(x_0) = \mathbb A^1_{\bar k} \subset
109	  \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$ the morphism $\varphi_{(s_0, s_1)}$ is the map ``$x
110	  \mapsto s_1(x) / s_0(x)$''; on the chart $D_+(x_1)$ it is the map ``$x
111	  \mapsto s_0(x) / s_1(x)$''; the two definitions agree on the overlap
112	  $D_+(x_0) \cap D_+(x_1) = \mathbb G_{m, \bar k}$ by the cocycle identity
113	  $(s_1 / s_0) \cdot (s_0 / s_1) = 1$.
114	\end{lemma}
115	
116	\begin{proof}
117	  \uses{def:codim1_cycles}
118	  This is the standard linear-system construction in the form of
119	  Hartshorne's Theorem~II.7.1 (``Morphisms to $\mathbb P^n$''), specialised to
120	  $n = 1$. Mathlib (snapshot \texttt{b80f227}) ships the corresponding API
121	  under the name
122	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Proj.fromOfGlobalSections} in
123	  \texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.ProjectiveSpectrum.Basic}:
124	
125	  \begin{quote}
126	  \small
127	  \texttt{def Proj.fromOfGlobalSections \{$\sigma$ A\} [CommRing A]
128	    [SetLike $\sigma$ A] [AddSubgroupClass $\sigma$ A] ($\mathcal A$ :
129	    \(\mathbb N\) → $\sigma$) [GradedRing $\mathcal A$] \{X : Scheme\}\\
130	    \phantom{}\quad(f : A →+* X.presheaf.obj $\top$)\\
131	    \phantom{}\quad(hf : Ideal.map f (HomogeneousIdeal.irrelevant $\mathcal A$).toIdeal = $\top$) :\\
132	    \phantom{}\quad(X $\longrightarrow$ Proj $\mathcal A$)}
133	  \end{quote}
134	
135	  Specialising to $\mathcal A = \bar k[x_0, x_1]$ with the standard $\mathbb
136	  N$-grading by total degree, the inputs are a graded $\bar k$-algebra
137	  homomorphism $f \colon \bar k[x_0, x_1] \to H^0(X, \mathcal O_X)$ and a
138	  proof that the irrelevant ideal $(x_0, x_1) \cdot \bar k[x_0, x_1]$ maps to
139	  the unit ideal of $H^0(X, \mathcal O_X)$ (i.e.\ no common zeros). The
140	  resulting morphism lands in $\mathrm{Proj}\,\bar k[x_0, x_1] = \mathbb
141	  P^1_{\bar k}$ (Hartshorne~II, Example~7.1.3, the construction of $\mathbb
142	  P^n$ as a $\mathrm{Proj}$). When $\mathcal L$ is non-trivial the present
143	  lemma's signature is reduced to the Mathlib API by working on the open
144	  cover $X = D(s_0) \cup D(s_1)$, where on each chart the sections become
145	  honest global functions ($s_1 / s_0$ on $D(s_0)$, $s_0 / s_1$ on $D(s_1)$,
146	  agreeing on the overlap). The chart-glue is performed by the
147	  project-bespoke wrapper
148	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.morphismToP1OfGlobalSections}, whose body
149	  in \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/RationalCurveIso.lean} chains
150	  \texttt{Proj.fromOfGlobalSections} with the chart-restriction-and-glue
151	  pattern of \texttt{Scheme.Cover.glueMorphisms}.
152	
153	  Uniqueness follows from the standard separation argument for morphisms to
154	  a separated scheme: two morphisms $\varphi, \psi \colon X \to \mathbb P^1$
155	  with $\varphi^* x_i = \psi^* x_i$ for $i = 0, 1$ agree on the basic-open
156	  charts $D_+(x_0), D_+(x_1)$ (where they pull back the affine coordinates to
157	  the same rational function), and these charts cover $\mathbb P^1$, so the
158	  two morphisms agree globally by gluing.
159	\end{proof}
160	
161	\section{Degree of a morphism to $\mathbb P^1$ via its pole divisor}
162	
163	\begin{lemma}
164	[Degree of a non-constant morphism to $\mathbb P^1$ via the preimage of $\infty$]
165	  \label{lem:degree_via_pole_divisor}
166	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.morphism_degree_via_pole_divisor}
167	  \uses{def:divisor_degree, def:order_at_point, def:divisor_closed_point}
168	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV \S2, opening paragraph of "Hurwitz's Theorem"
169	  % (definition of the degree of a finite morphism of curves) (read from
170	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 316, doc p.~299).
171	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Recall that the \emph{degree} of a finite morphism
172	  % $f: X \to Y$ of curves is defined to be the degree $[K(X):K(Y)]$ of the
173	  % extension of function fields (II, \S 6)."
174	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.2, p.~299, opening of \S2.}
175	  Let $\varphi \colon C \to \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$ be a non-constant morphism
176	  from a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve $C / \bar k$. Then
177	  $\varphi$ is finite, its function-field extension
178	  $\bar k(\mathbb P^1) \hookrightarrow K(C)$ is finite, and the degree of
179	  $\varphi$
180	  \[
181	    \deg(\varphi) \;:=\; [K(C) : \bar k(\mathbb P^1)] \;\in\; \mathbb N_{>0}
182	  \]
183	  is recovered as the degree of the pullback of any closed point of $\mathbb
184	  P^1$: for every closed point $Q \in \mathbb P^1$,
185	  \[
186	    \deg(\varphi^*[Q]) \;=\; \deg(\varphi).
187	  \]
188	  In particular, taking $Q = \infty \in \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$, the morphism
189	  satisfies
190	  \[
191	    \deg(\varphi) \;=\;
192	    \deg\!\left(\,\sum_{P \in \varphi^{-1}(\infty)}
193	                  \ord_P(\varphi^* t_\infty) \cdot [P]\,\right),
194	  \]
195	  where $t_\infty \in K(\mathbb P^1)$ is a local parameter at $\infty$
196	  (equivalently $t_\infty = 1/u$ for the standard affine coordinate $u$ on
197	  $\mathbb P^1 \setminus \{\infty\}$).
198	\end{lemma}
199	
200	\begin{proof}
201	  \uses{def:divisor_degree, def:order_at_point, def:divisor_closed_point,
202	        thm:principal_deg_zero}
203	  A non-constant morphism between smooth proper curves over $\bar k$ is
204	  automatically finite. Indeed, the image of $\varphi$ is a closed
205	  irreducible subset of $\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$ that is not a point (else
206	  $\varphi$ would be constant) and not empty, so it is all of $\mathbb
207	  P^1_{\bar k}$ (the only proper irreducible closed subset of $\mathbb
208	  P^1_{\bar k}$ is a point); hence $\varphi$ is surjective. A surjective
209	  morphism between smooth proper irreducible curves of equal dimension is
210	  finite (proper + quasi-finite, where quasi-finiteness comes from the
211	  fact that $\varphi$ has zero-dimensional fibres: a positive-dimensional
212	  fibre would force a one-dimensional irreducible component in the fibre,
213	  contradicting irreducibility of $\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$ and $C$ at
214	  generic points). Hence the function-field extension
215	  $\bar k(\mathbb P^1) = \varphi^\# K(\mathbb P^1) \hookrightarrow K(C)$
216	  is finite.
217	
218	  The identification $\deg(\varphi^*[Q]) = [K(C) : \bar k(\mathbb P^1)]$ for
219	  any closed point $Q \in \mathbb P^1$ is the multiplicativity of the
220	  degree under finite pullback (Hartshorne~II.6.9): for a finite morphism
221	  $\varphi \colon C \to C'$ of smooth proper curves and any divisor $D \in
222	  \Div(C')$, $\deg(\varphi^* D) = \deg(\varphi) \cdot \deg(D)$. Specialising
223	  to $D = [Q]$ (a divisor of degree $1$ since $\kappa(Q) = \bar k$) gives
224	  $\deg(\varphi^*[Q]) = \deg(\varphi)$. Equivalently, the degree of
225	  $\varphi$ equals the number of preimages of any closed point of $\mathbb
226	  P^1$, counted with the ramification multiplicities $\ord_P(\varphi^*
227	  t_\infty)$ at the preimage points $P \in \varphi^{-1}(\infty)$.
228	
229	  \emph{Sub-build note.} The lemma threads two auxiliary inputs:
230	  ``non-constant morphism between smooth proper curves is finite'' (the
231	  surjective-and-proper-and-quasi-finite argument above; Hartshorne~II.6.8)
232	  and ``multiplicativity of degree under finite pullback'' (Hartshorne~II.6.9
233	  and the multiplicativity of $\deg$ under composition of $\div$ and
234	  $\deg$; the closely related ``$\div(f)$ has degree zero'' specialisation
235	  is \Cref{thm:principal_deg_zero} of \texttt{RR.1}). Both are separable
236	  sub-lemmas inside \texttt{RR.4}'s Lean file
237	  (\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/RationalCurveIso.lean}) and are
238	  slotted for closure in a follow-up iteration after the
239	  \texttt{morphism\_degree\_via\_pole\_divisor} skeleton lands.
240	\end{proof}

242	\section{A degree-$1$ morphism between smooth proper curves is an isomorphism}
243	
244	\begin{lemma}
245	[Degree-$1$ morphism between smooth proper curves is an isomorphism]
246	  \label{lem:degree_one_morphism_iso}
247	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.iso_of_degree_one}
248	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], I \S6 Corollary~6.12 (function-field-to-curve
249	  % equivalence of categories) (read from
250	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 62, doc p.~45)
251	  % + [Hartshorne], IV \S2 opening paragraph (degree of a finite morphism
252	  % of curves is the function-field extension degree) (read from
253	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 316, doc p.~299).
254	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Corollary 6.12. \emph{The following three categories are
255	  % equivalent:}
256	  % (i) \emph{nonsingular projective curves, and dominant morphisms;}
257	  % (ii) \emph{quasi-projective curves, and dominant rational maps;}
258	  % (iii) \emph{function fields of dimension 1 over $k$, and $k$-homomorphisms.}"
259	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Recall that the \emph{degree} of a finite morphism
260	  % $f: X \to Y$ of curves is defined to be the degree $[K(X):K(Y)]$ of the
261	  % extension of function fields (II, \S 6)."
262	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, I.6 Corollary~6.12 (p.~45) + IV.2 opening
263	  (p.~299).}
264	  Let $C, C'$ be smooth proper geometrically irreducible curves over an
265	  algebraically closed field $\bar k$, and let $\varphi \colon C \to C'$ be
266	  a non-constant morphism with $\deg(\varphi) = 1$. Then $\varphi$ is an
267	  isomorphism of $\bar k$-schemes.
268	\end{lemma}
269	
270	\begin{proof}
271	  By \Cref{lem:degree_via_pole_divisor} (specialised to a non-constant
272	  morphism, not necessarily to $\mathbb P^1$; the same argument applies to
273	  any non-constant morphism between smooth proper curves over $\bar k$),
274	  the morphism $\varphi$ is finite and the function-field extension
275	  $\varphi^\# \colon \bar k(C') \hookrightarrow \bar k(C)$ has degree
276	  $[\bar k(C) : \bar k(C')] = \deg(\varphi) = 1$. Therefore $\varphi^\#$
277	  is an isomorphism of function fields $\bar k(C') \cong \bar k(C)$.
278	
279	  By Hartshorne's Corollary~I.6.12, the assignment $C \mapsto \bar k(C)$ is
280	  an equivalence of categories between smooth projective curves over $\bar
281	  k$ (with dominant morphisms) and function fields of transcendence degree
282	  one over $\bar k$ (with $\bar k$-homomorphisms). Under this equivalence,
283	  the isomorphism $\bar k(C') \cong \bar k(C)$ produced by the previous
284	  paragraph lifts to a unique isomorphism of smooth proper curves $C \to
285	  C'$, and this isomorphism agrees with $\varphi$ on the generic point (by
286	  construction: it is the inverse-image of $\varphi^\#$ under the
287	  equivalence). The two morphisms $C \to C'$ that agree on the generic
288	  point of the integral scheme $C$ agree everywhere (a morphism out of an
289	  integral scheme to a separated scheme is determined by its restriction
290	  to the generic point, Hartshorne~II Ex.~4.2). Hence $\varphi$ is itself
291	  the lift, and is therefore an isomorphism.
292	
293	  \emph{Alternative direct argument.} Without invoking the
294	  equivalence-of-categories framing, the same conclusion is obtained
295	  scheme-theoretically: a finite degree-$1$ morphism $\varphi \colon C \to
296	  C'$ between integral normal noetherian schemes is an isomorphism.
297	  Indeed, the direct image $\varphi_* \mathcal O_C$ is a coherent
298	  $\mathcal O_{C'}$-module that is generically of rank $\deg(\varphi) =
299	  1$, and an inclusion $\mathcal O_{C'} \hookrightarrow \varphi_*
300	  \mathcal O_C$ of coherent rank-$1$ sheaves whose generic ranks agree
301	  is an isomorphism over the locus where the quotient
302	  $\varphi_* \mathcal O_C / \mathcal O_{C'}$ has trivial stalk. The
303	  quotient is a torsion coherent $\mathcal O_{C'}$-module of generic rank
304	  $\deg(\varphi) - 1 = 0$, hence is itself torsion-free (a
305	  finitely-generated torsion module over a Dedekind domain of generic
306	  rank zero is zero), so vanishes. Therefore $\varphi_* \mathcal O_C =
307	  \mathcal O_{C'}$ and $\varphi$ is the structure morphism of an
308	  isomorphism. Cf.\ Stacks tag~\textbf{0AVX} for the categorical version.
309	
310	  \emph{Sub-build note.} The Lean signature
311	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.iso\_of\_degree\_one} consumes a
312	  non-constant finite morphism $\varphi \colon C \to C'$ together with the
313	  degree-$1$ hypothesis $\deg(\varphi) = 1$ and produces an isomorphism
314	  $C \cong C'$ in the slice category $\mathtt{Over (Spec (.of\ kbar))}$.
315	  The proof body threads the Mathlib API
316	  \texttt{IsIntegralHom.iso\_of\_isUnit} (or the equivalent
317	  \texttt{IsAffineHom + Finite + RankOne} chain) and, where needed, the
318	  project-bespoke ``finite-morphism-of-degree-one is an isomorphism''
319	  helper. The Hartshorne~I.6.12 equivalence-of-categories framing is the
320	  intended high-level proof structure; the scheme-theoretic argument
321	  above is the spelled-out form for the Lean body.
322	\end{proof}

324	\section{Headline: genus-$0$ curve over $\bar k$ is isomorphic to $\mathbb P^1$}
325	
326	\begin{theorem}
327	
328	\leanok
329	[Genus-$0$ curve over $\bar k$ is isomorphic to $\mathbb P^1$]
330	  \label{thm:genus_zero_curve_iso_p1}
331	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.genusZero_curve_iso_P1}
332	  \uses{def:genus, lem:morphism_to_p1_from_global_sections,
333	        lem:degree_via_pole_divisor, lem:degree_one_morphism_iso,
334	        cor:nonconstant_function_genus_zero,
335	        thm:riemannRoch_genus_zero,
336	        def:codim1_cycles, def:divisor_degree}
337	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV \S1 Example~1.3.5 (genus-zero classification;
338	  % "rational $\iff g = 0$" + "rational curve $\cong \mathbb P^1$ for
339	  % complete nonsingular curves") (read from
340	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 314, doc p.~297).
341	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Example 1.3.5. Recall that a curve is \emph{rational}
342	  % if it is birational to $\mathbf P^1$ (I, Ex. 6.1). Since curves in this
343	  % chapter are complete and nonsingular by definition, a curve $X$ is
344	  % rational if and only if $X \cong \mathbf P^1$ (I, 6.12). Now using
345	  % (1.3) we can show that $X$ is rational if and only if $g = 0$. We
346	  % already know that $p_a(\mathbf P^1) = 0$ (I, Ex. 7.2), so conversely
347	  % suppose given a curve $X$ of genus 0. Let $P, Q$ be two distinct
348	  % points of $X$ and apply Riemann-Roch to the divisor $D = P - Q$.
349	  % Since $\deg(K - D) = -2$, using (1.3.3) above, we have $l(K - D) = 0$,
350	  % and so we find $l(D) = 1$. But $D$ is a divisor of degree 0, so by
351	  % (1.2) we have $D \sim 0$, in other words $P \sim Q$. But this implies
352	  % $X$ is rational (II, 6.10.1)."
353	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1, Example~1.3.5, p.~297.}
354	  Let $\bar k$ be an algebraically closed field, and let $C$ be a smooth
355	  proper geometrically irreducible curve over $\bar k$ with $g(C) = 0$.
356	  Then there exists an isomorphism of $\bar k$-schemes
357	  \[
358	    \varphi \colon C \stackrel{\sim}{\longrightarrow} \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}.
359	  \]
360	  Concretely, the Lean signature
361	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.genusZero\_curve\_iso\_P1} (the existing pinned
362	  target at \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/AbelianVarietyRigidity.lean:290}) is
363	  closed by the proof below; the existing interface block
364	  \Cref{prop:genusZero_curve_iso_P1} of
365	  \Cref{chap:AbelianVarietyRigidity} is its consumer-side restatement,
366	  pinning the same Lean declaration.
367	\end{theorem}
368	
369	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the verbatim Example IV.1.3.5 text in the
370	% statement block above (Hartshorne states the classification and its
371	% proof inside the single Example; the proof is the second half beginning
372	% "We already know that $p_a(\mathbf P^1) = 0$ \ldots"). The present
373	% proof body restates Hartshorne's argument in the project's notation
374	% (linear-system construction $C \to \mathbb P^1$ via $H^0(C, \mathcal O_C
375	% ([P]))$, degree-$1$ identification, isomorphism).
376	\begin{proof}
377	  \uses{def:genus, lem:morphism_to_p1_from_global_sections,
378	        lem:degree_via_pole_divisor, lem:degree_one_morphism_iso,
379	        cor:nonconstant_function_genus_zero,
380	        thm:riemannRoch_genus_zero,
381	        def:codim1_cycles, def:divisor_degree}
382	  Over the algebraically closed field $\bar k$ the smooth proper
383	  geometrically irreducible curve $C$ has closed points, all of which are
384	  $\bar k$-rational (the residue field of a closed point of a curve over
385	  $\bar k$ is a finite extension of $\bar k$, hence is $\bar k$ itself by
386	  algebraic closedness). Fix one such $\bar k$-rational point $P \in C$;
387	  this is the marked point used to build the linear system below.
388	
389	  \emph{Step 1: non-constant function $f \in H^0(C, \mathcal O_C([P]))$.}
390	  By Hartshorne's Riemann--Roch in genus zero
391	  (\Cref{thm:riemannRoch_genus_zero} of \texttt{RR.2}) applied to the
392	  effective divisor $[P]$ of degree $1$, we obtain
393	  $\ell([P]) = \deg([P]) + 1 = 2$. The constant section
394	  $1 \in \bar k \cdot 1 \subset H^0(C, \mathcal O_C([P]))$ accounts for a
395	  one-dimensional $\bar k$-subspace; the two-dimensional quotient supplies
396	  a non-constant section $f \in H^0(C, \mathcal O_C([P])) \setminus
397	  \bar k \cdot 1$. This is the corollary
398	  \texttt{cor:nonconstant\_function\_genus\_zero} of \texttt{RR.3} (sibling
399	  chapter, \texttt{RiemannRoch\_OCofP.tex}).
400	
401	  \emph{Step 2: morphism $C \to \mathbb P^1$ via the linear system $(1, f)$.}
402	  The pair $(1, f) \in H^0(C, \mathcal O_C([P]))^2$ generates the
403	  invertible sheaf $\mathcal O_C([P])$ at every point of $C$: at points
404	  $Q \in C \setminus \{P\}$ the section $1$ does not vanish in
405	  $\mathcal O_C([P])_Q \cong \mathcal O_{C, Q}$, and at the marked point
406	  $P$ the section $f$ does not vanish in $\mathcal O_C([P])_P$ (because $f$
407	  has a pole of order exactly $1$ at $P$ in the rational-function
408	  picture: $f \in K(C) \setminus \bar k$ with $\div(f)_\infty = [P]$).
409	  Therefore by \Cref{lem:morphism_to_p1_from_global_sections} there
410	  exists a unique morphism
411	  \[
412	    \varphi \;:=\; \varphi_{(1, f)} \colon C \longrightarrow \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}
413	  \]
414	  with $\varphi^* x_0 = 1$ and $\varphi^* x_1 = f$ (so the pullback of
415	  the standard ratio coordinate $u = x_1 / x_0$ on $\mathbb P^1 \setminus
416	  \{\infty\}$ is $f$, and the pullback of the inverse coordinate
417	  $t_\infty = x_0 / x_1 = 1 / f$ is the local parameter $1/f$ at $P$).
418	
419	  \emph{Step 3: $\deg(\varphi) = 1$ via the pole divisor of $f$.} The
420	  morphism $\varphi$ is non-constant (since $f$ is non-constant as a
421	  rational function on $C$, the pullback $\varphi^* u = f$ takes
422	  infinitely many values, ruling out a constant $\varphi$). By
423	  \Cref{lem:degree_via_pole_divisor} applied to $\varphi$,
424	  \[
425	    \deg(\varphi)
426	    \;=\;
427	    \deg\!\left(\,\sum_{Q \in \varphi^{-1}(\infty)}
428	                  \ord_Q(\varphi^* t_\infty) \cdot [Q]\,\right).
429	  \]
430	  We compute the pole divisor of $f$ as a rational function on $C$:
431	  since $f \in H^0(C, \mathcal O_C([P]))$ with $f$ non-constant and
432	  $\mathcal O_C([P])$ the line bundle of the effective divisor $[P]$, the
433	  rational-function $f$ has pole divisor $(f)_\infty = [P]$ (the
434	  unique pole of order $1$ at $P$, no other poles; this is the standard
435	  identification of $H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(D))$ with the rational functions
436	  $g \in K(C)$ satisfying $\div(g) + D \geq 0$, specialised to
437	  $D = [P]$). Hence $\varphi^{-1}(\infty) = \{P\}$ with multiplicity~$1$
438	  and
439	  \[
440	    \deg(\varphi) \;=\; \deg([P]) \;=\; 1.
441	  \]
442	
443	  \emph{Step 4: $\varphi$ is an isomorphism.} By
444	  \Cref{lem:degree_one_morphism_iso} applied to the non-constant degree-$1$
445	  morphism $\varphi \colon C \to \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$ (smooth proper
446	  geometrically irreducible curves over $\bar k$ on both sides), $\varphi$
447	  is an isomorphism of $\bar k$-schemes.
448	
449	  This produces the required isomorphism $C \cong \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$
450	  closing the headline target
451	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.genusZero\_curve\_iso\_P1}.
452	
453	  \emph{Comparison with Hartshorne's original argument.} Hartshorne's
454	  Example~IV.1.3.5 phrases the argument in terms of two distinct points
455	  $P, Q \in C$ and the degree-zero divisor $D = P - Q$, deriving $P \sim Q$
456	  and concluding via II.6.10.1 that $C$ is rational; combined with I.6.12
457	  this yields $C \cong \mathbb P^1$. The present proof restates the same
458	  combinatorial content via the more directly formalisable linear-system
459	  construction: instead of going through the $P \sim Q$ statement and
460	  invoking II.6.10.1 as a black box, we produce the explicit morphism
461	  $\varphi \colon C \to \mathbb P^1$ from the two sections $(1, f)$ of
462	  $\mathcal O_C([P])$ given by Riemann--Roch and identify its degree
463	  directly. The two arguments are mathematically equivalent and have the
464	  same dependency footprint (Riemann--Roch in genus zero $\Rightarrow$
465	  $\ell([P]) = 2$ $\Rightarrow$ non-constant $f$ $\Rightarrow$ degree-$1$
466	  map to $\mathbb P^1$ $\Rightarrow$ iso); the linear-system phrasing maps
467	  more cleanly onto the Mathlib \texttt{Proj.fromOfGlobalSections} API and
468	  the project's existing Weil-divisor/degree pipeline of \texttt{RR.1}.
469	\end{proof}

493	    genus $0$ over a \emph{non-closed} field $k$ with a $k$-rational point
494	    $\cong \mathbb P^1_k$'' statement is the $\bar k \to k$ descent of

513	    $\bar k$ with dominant morphisms'' $\cong$ ``function fields of

515	    (Hartshorne~I.6.12) is consumed as a high-level framing in

517	    the equivalence-of-categories level here. The proof body in the Lean

521	    pin compatible with the chapter's downstream needs.
522	  \item ``A smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over $\bar k$

11	# The line bundle `𝒪_C(P)` of a closed point and its global sections (RR.3)
12	
13	This file is the **RR.3** sub-build chapter for the project's headline
14	`genusZero_curve_iso_P1` (the "smooth proper geometrically irreducible
15	genus-`0` curve over `k̄` is isomorphic to `ℙ¹`" lemma in
16	`AlgebraicJacobian.AbelianVarietyRigidity`).
17	
18	The Hartshorne IV.1.3.5 chain for the genus-`0` ↦ `ℙ¹` classification routes
19	through:
20	
21	- `RR.1` (`WeilDivisor.lean`): the Weil divisor group `Div(C)` and the
22	  degree map `deg : Div(C) → ℤ`.
23	- `RR.2` (`RRFormula.lean`): the Euler-characteristic identity
24	  `χ(𝒪_C(D)) = deg(D) + 1 − g` and the Riemann–Roch dimension formula
25	  `ℓ(D) = deg(D) + 1` in genus `0`.
26	- **`RR.3` (this file)**: the invertible sheaf `𝒪_C(P)` of a closed point
27	  `P ∈ C`, its `k̄`-vector space of global sections as the
28	  Riemann–Roch space `L([P])`, the `H¹`-vanishing
29	  `H¹(C, 𝒪_C(P)) = 0` on a genus-`0` curve, the dimension formula

62	/-! ## §1. Codim-1 cycle group / Weil divisor group
63	
64	Hartshorne's condition `(*)` (II §6, p. 130) is: `X` is a Noetherian integral
65	separated scheme that is regular in codimension one. Under `(*)`, prime divisors
66	are closed integral subschemes of codimension one, and the local ring at the
67	generic point of a prime divisor is a DVR with quotient field the function field
68	`K(X)`.
69	
70	A prime divisor is encoded by its **generic point** together with the
71	codimension-one witness `Order.coheight point = 1`: the data field `point : X`
72	selects the generic point of the closed integral subscheme, and the predicate
73	field `coheight` enforces codimension one in the specialisation preorder
74	(Hartshorne II §6 p. 130; blueprint pin `def:prime_divisor`). Integrality of the
75	closure is automatic, so no separate integrality witness is needed.
76	-/
77	
78	/-- A **prime divisor** on a scheme `X`: a closed integral subscheme of codimension
79	one, encoded (for a scheme satisfying Hartshorne's condition `(*)`) by its generic
80	point. On a curve, prime divisors correspond bijectively to closed points.
81	
82	The codimension-one witness is the predicate `Order.coheight point = 1` on the
83	specialisation preorder of `X.carrier` (Mathlib's convention is
84	`x ≤ y ↔ y ⤳ x`, so the generic point is the unique maximal element and a
85	generic point of a codim-1 closed integral subscheme has a length-one chain
86	above it). The closure `{point}̄` is automatically irreducible (so the reduced
87	induced subscheme structure is automatically integral); hence no separate
88	integrality field is needed.
89	
90	Blueprint reference: `def:prime_divisor` / `def:codim1_cycles` (Hartshorne II §6
91	p. 130; Stacks 02RW). -/
92	structure Scheme.PrimeDivisor (X : Scheme.{u}) where
93	  /-- The generic point of the closed integral subscheme. -/
94	  point : X
95	  /-- Codimension-1 witness: `point` has coheight `1` in the specialisation
96	  preorder on `X.carrier`. Per iter-173 `wd-spec-refine` (`def:prime_divisor`). -/
97	  coheight : Order.coheight point = 1
98	
99	/-- The **Weil divisor group** of a scheme `X` satisfying Hartshorne's condition

101	`Div(X) = ⨁_{Y prime divisor of X} ℤ · Y`. An element `D = Σ nᵢ · Yᵢ` with finitely
102	many nonzero coefficients is a **Weil divisor**; if all `nᵢ ≥ 0` it is **effective**.
103	
104	Blueprint reference: `def:codim1_cycles` (Hartshorne II §6 p. 130). -/
105	def Scheme.WeilDivisor (X : Scheme.{u}) : Type u := X.PrimeDivisor →₀ ℤ

117	/-! ## §2. Order of a rational function at a prime divisor
118	
119	For a scheme `X` satisfying `(*)`, every prime divisor `Y` carries a discrete
120	valuation `v_Y` on the function field `K(X)`. The order of a nonzero rational
121	function `f ∈ K(X)^×` along `Y` is the integer `ord_Y(f) := v_Y(f)`. -/
122	
123	namespace Scheme.RationalMap
124	
125	/-- **Order of a nonzero rational function `f ∈ K(X)^×` along a prime divisor `Y`.**
126	
127	For `X` satisfying Hartshorne's `(*)`, the local ring `O_{X,η}` at the generic
128	point `η` of `Y` is a discrete valuation ring with quotient field the function
129	field `K(X)`, and `ord_Y(f) = v_Y(f) ∈ ℤ` is the value of the associated normalised
130	discrete valuation on `f`.
131	
132	On a smooth proper curve `C` over `k̄`, every closed point `P ∈ C` is a prime
133	divisor and `ord_P(f) = v_P(f)` is the standard DVR valuation at `P`.
134	
135	Blueprint reference: `def:order_at_point` (Hartshorne II §6 pp. 130–131; Stacks 02ME).
136	
137	iter-176 body (per analogist `dvr-rationalmap-order`): the body uses Mathlib's
138	`Ring.ordFrac` (the canonical `K →*₀ ℤᵐ⁰` monoid-with-zero hom from
139	`Mathlib.RingTheory.OrderOfVanishing.Basic`, Stacks `02MD`) on the stalk
140	`X.presheaf.stalk Y.point`, then projects through `WithZero.log : ℤᵐ⁰ → ℤ`
141	(the canonical projection with junk-on-zero, `Mathlib.Algebra.GroupWithZero.WithZero`).
142	On `f = 0` this gives `order Y 0 = 0` (junk convention from `WithZero.log_zero`).
143	
144	The required Mathlib typeclasses on the stalk are:
145	- `IsNoetherianRing` — from `[IsLocallyNoetherian X]`.
146	- `IsDomain` ⟹ `Nontrivial` — from `[IsIntegral X]`.
147	- `IsFractionRing (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point) X.functionField` — from `[IsIntegral X]`.
148	- `Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)` — threaded explicitly (the
149	  topological-coheight-to-Krull-dim bridge `Order.coheight Y.point = 1 ⟹
150	  Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)` is a Mathlib-upstream-pending
151	  gap; see Stacks `02IZ` / `005X`). -/
152	noncomputable def order {X : Scheme.{u}} [IsIntegral X]
153	    [IsLocallyNoetherian X] (Y : X.PrimeDivisor)
154	    [Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)]
155	    (f : X.functionField) : ℤ :=
156	  WithZero.log (Ring.ordFrac (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point) f)
157	
158	end Scheme.RationalMap

164	/-! ## §3. Divisor of a closed point on a curve
165	
166	On a smooth proper curve `C` over a field, every closed point `P ∈ C` is a prime
167	divisor (it is closed, integral, of codimension one in the one-dimensional integral
168	scheme `C`). The associated Weil divisor is `[P] = 1 · P ∈ Div(C)`. -/
169	
170	/-- **The Weil divisor associated to a closed point `P` on a curve.** The element
171	`[P] := 1 · P ∈ Div(C)`, i.e. the prime divisor `P` with multiplicity one.
172	
173	For a smooth proper curve every closed point is automatically a prime divisor (it
174	is a codimension-one integral closed subscheme of the one-dimensional integral
175	scheme `C`); conversely every prime divisor on a curve is of this form, so an
176	arbitrary divisor on `C` is a finite formal `ℤ`-linear combination
177	`Σ nᵢ · [Pᵢ]` of closed points.
178	
179	Blueprint reference: `def:divisor_closed_point` (Hartshorne II §6 p. 137).
180	
181	iter-174 body: the function is junk-defined outside its intended scope. We
182	case-split on `Order.coheight P = 1` (the codimension-one witness of
183	`PrimeDivisor`). On the branch where the equality holds — automatic for a
184	closed point on a one-dimensional integral scheme — we promote `P` to a
185	`PrimeDivisor` via the witness and return `Finsupp.single ⟨P, h⟩ 1`, i.e.
186	the prime divisor `P` with multiplicity one. On the off-branch (junk regime)
187	we return the zero divisor. The blueprint pins the well-definedness argument
188	"`IsClosed {P}` on a one-dimensional integral scheme ⟹ `coheight P = 1`" as a
189	separate threadable hypothesis at the call site (chapter L330–L340 "Lean
190	signature scope"); see `ofClosedPoint_eq_single` for the equation in the
191	hypothesised regime. -/
192	noncomputable def ofClosedPoint {C : Scheme.{u}} (P : C)
193	    (_hP : IsClosed ({P} : Set C)) : C.WeilDivisor :=
194	  if h : Order.coheight P = 1 then Finsupp.single ⟨P, h⟩ 1 else 0
195	
196	/-- In the hypothesised regime where the closed point `P` has coheight one
197	(the codim-1 condition automatic for a closed point of a one-dimensional
198	integral scheme), `ofClosedPoint P hP` is the prime divisor `P` with
199	multiplicity one. -/
200	lemma ofClosedPoint_eq_single {C : Scheme.{u}} (P : C)
201	    (hP : IsClosed ({P} : Set C)) (h : Order.coheight P = 1) :
202	    ofClosedPoint P hP = Finsupp.single ⟨P, h⟩ 1 := by
203	  simp [ofClosedPoint, h]
204	
205	/-- Off-branch: outside the codim-1 regime, `ofClosedPoint` is junk-defined as
206	the zero divisor. (Only relevant when the user supplies a "closed point" that
207	does not have coheight one in the ambient scheme — e.g. a generic point or a
208	codim-≥2 point on a higher-dimensional scheme.) -/
209	lemma ofClosedPoint_eq_zero {C : Scheme.{u}} (P : C)
210	    (hP : IsClosed ({P} : Set C)) (h : Order.coheight P ≠ 1) :
211	    ofClosedPoint P hP = 0 := by
212	  simp [ofClosedPoint, h]
213	
214	/-! ## §4. Degree of a divisor over an algebraically closed base -/
215	
216	/-- **Degree of a Weil divisor on a smooth proper curve over `k̄`.**
217	
218	Over an algebraically closed field `k̄`, every closed point of a smooth proper
219	curve `C` has residue field `k̄`, so each prime divisor `[P]` contributes degree
220	one to the sum, and `deg(D) := Σᵢ nᵢ` is the sum of the integer coefficients of
221	the formal sum `D = Σ nᵢ · [Pᵢ]`.
222	
223	(Over a general field `k` one weights by the residue-field degrees
224	`Σᵢ nᵢ · [κ(Pᵢ) : k]` to recover the geometric degree; the project's RR bridge
225	needs only the `k̄` specialisation.)
226	
227	Blueprint reference: `def:divisor_degree` (Hartshorne II §6 p. 137; Stacks 0BE0).
228	
229	Implementation: `Finsupp.sum D (fun _ n => n)` is the sum of all coefficients of
230	the finitely supported function representing `D`. -/
231	noncomputable def degree (D : X.WeilDivisor) : ℤ :=
232	  (D : X.PrimeDivisor →₀ ℤ).sum (fun _ n => n)
233	
234	/-- **The degree map is a group homomorphism `Div(C) → ℤ`.**
235	
236	`deg(D₁ + D₂) = deg(D₁) + deg(D₂)`, `deg(-D) = -deg(D)`, `deg(0) = 0`. Bundled
237	as an `AddMonoidHom` for downstream use (the linear-equivalence quotient
238	`Cl(C) := Div(C) / im(div)` will inherit a `deg : Cl(C) → ℤ` from this).
239	
240	Blueprint reference: `thm:divisor_degree_hom` (immediate from `def:divisor_degree`).
241	
242	Implementation: built from `Finsupp.liftAddHom (fun _ ↦ AddMonoidHom.id ℤ)`, the
243	generic Mathlib packaging that lifts a family of `AddMonoidHom`s indexed by the
244	support into a single `AddMonoidHom` on the finsupp. Unfolds to
245	`D.sum (fun _ z ↦ z) = degree D` (see `degree_hom_apply` below). -/
246	noncomputable def degree_hom : X.WeilDivisor →+ ℤ :=
247	  Finsupp.liftAddHom (fun _ ↦ AddMonoidHom.id ℤ)
248	
249	@[simp]
250	lemma degree_hom_apply (D : X.WeilDivisor) : degree_hom D = degree D :=
251	  Finsupp.liftAddHom_apply (α := X.PrimeDivisor) (M := ℤ) (N := ℤ)
252	    (fun _ ↦ AddMonoidHom.id ℤ) D

255	
256	/-- **The principal divisor of a nonzero rational function `f ∈ K(X)^×`.**
257	
258	By Hartshorne's Lemma 6.1, `ord_Y(f) = 0` for all but finitely many prime
259	divisors `Y`, so the formal sum
260	`div(f) := Σ_{Y prime divisor} ord_Y(f) · Y ∈ Div(X)`
261	has finite support and is a well-defined Weil divisor.

263	On a smooth proper curve `C` over `k̄`, this specialises to
264	`div(f) = Σ_{P closed point} ord_P(f) · [P]`.
265	
266	Blueprint reference: `def:principal_divisor` (Hartshorne II §6 Lemma 6.1 +
267	following definition, p. 131).

269	iter-173+: the body invokes Hartshorne 6.1 for the finite-support side condition,
270	then materialises the `Finsupp` whose value at each prime divisor `Y` is
271	`Scheme.RationalMap.order Y f`. -/
272	noncomputable def principal [IsIntegral X] (f : X.functionField)
273	    (_hf : f ≠ 0) : X.WeilDivisor :=
274	  sorry
275	
276	/-- **The principal-divisor map is a group homomorphism `K(X)^× → Div(X)`.**
277	
278	Concretely `div(fg) = div(f) + div(g)`, `div(f⁻¹) = -div(f)`, `div(1) = 0`. Bundled
279	as a `MonoidHom` from the multiplicative units of `K(X)` to `Multiplicative (Div(X))`
280	(equivalently: an additive map `(K(X)^×, ·) → (Div(X), +)`).
281	
282	Blueprint reference: `thm:principal_hom` (Hartshorne II §6 p. 131).
283	
284	iter-173+: closes coordinate-wise from the DVR identities `v_Y(fg) = v_Y(f) + v_Y(g)`,
285	`v_Y(f⁻¹) = -v_Y(f)`, `v_Y(1) = 0` (`AddValuation.map_add`, etc.), summing over the
286	union of supports. -/
287	noncomputable def principal_hom [IsIntegral X] :
288	    (X.functionField)ˣ →* Multiplicative X.WeilDivisor :=
289	  sorry
290	
291	/-- **Principal divisors on a complete nonsingular curve have degree zero**
292	(Hartshorne Corollary II.6.10, Stacks 0BE3).
293	
294	For every nonzero rational function `f ∈ K(C)^×` on a smooth proper curve `C`
295	over an algebraically closed field `k̄`,
296	`deg(div(f)) = 0 ∈ ℤ`.
297	
298	Blueprint reference: `thm:principal_deg_zero` (Hartshorne II.6 Cor. 6.10 p. 138).
299	
300	The proof (Hartshorne 6.10): if `f ∈ k̄` is constant then `div(f) = 0` and the
301	claim is trivial. Otherwise the inclusion `k̄(f) ⊂ K(C)` exhibits `K(C)` as a
302	finite extension of `k̄(f) ≅ k̄(t)`, so the corresponding morphism
303	`φ : C → ℙ¹_{k̄}` is finite, `div(f) = φ^*([0] - [∞])`, and pullback along a
304	finite morphism multiplies degree by `deg(φ)`. Two auxiliary sub-lemmas
305	(finite morphism induced by a non-constant rational function; multiplicativity
306	of degree under finite pullback, Hartshorne II.6.9) are deferred to follow-up
307	iters of `RR.1`. -/

309	    (C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))) [IsProper C.hom]
310	    [SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom] [GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom]
311	    [IsIntegral C.left] (f : C.left.functionField) (hf : f ≠ 0) :
312	    degree (principal f hf) = 0 :=
313	  sorry

315	/-! ## §6. Linear equivalence and the divisor class group -/
316	
317	/-- **Linear equivalence of Weil divisors.**
318	
319	Two Weil divisors `D, D' ∈ Div(X)` are linearly equivalent, written `D ~ D'`, if
320	and only if there exists a nonzero rational function `f ∈ K(X)^×` with
321	`D - D' = div(f) ∈ Div(X)`.
322	
323	`~` is an equivalence relation (reflexivity from `div(1) = 0`, symmetry from
324	`div(f⁻¹) = -div(f)`, transitivity from `div(fg) = div(f) + div(g)`, all via
325	`thm:principal_hom`). The quotient `Cl(X) := Div(X) / im(div)` is the
326	**divisor class group** of `X`.
327	
328	On a smooth proper curve `C` over `k̄`, `thm:principal_deg_zero` shows the
329	degree map descends to `deg : Cl(C) → ℤ`.
330	
331	Blueprint reference: `def:linear_equivalence` (Hartshorne II §6 p. 131). -/
332	def LinearEquivalence [IsIntegral X] (D D' : X.WeilDivisor) : Prop :=
333	  ∃ (f : X.functionField) (hf : f ≠ 0), D - D' = principal f hf
334	
335	end Scheme.WeilDivisor

115	/-! ## §1. The indeterminacy locus of a rational map
116	
117	The indeterminacy locus `Z(f) ⊆ X` of a rational map `f : X ⇢ Y` is the closed
118	complement of its domain of definition `Dom(f) := f.domain` (a Mathlib-shipped
119	open subset of `X`).
120	
121	Blueprint pin: `def:indeterminacy_locus` (Milne §I.3 p. 16). -/
122	
123	/-- **Indeterminacy locus of a rational map.** The closed complement of the
124	domain of definition `f.domain` (an `X.Opens`, exposed by Mathlib at
125	`Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Birational.RationalMap`):
126	
127	`Z(f) := X ∖ Dom(f) ⊆ X`.
128	
129	`f` is everywhere defined (i.e. extends to a regular morphism) iff
130	`indeterminacyLocus f = ∅`.
131	
132	Blueprint reference: `def:indeterminacy_locus` (Milne, *Abelian Varieties*,
133	§I.3, p. 16). -/
134	def indeterminacyLocus {X Y : Scheme.{u}} (f : X.RationalMap Y) : Set X :=
135	  (f.domain : Set X)ᶜ
136	
137	/-- The indeterminacy locus of a rational map is closed (it is the complement
138	of an open). -/
139	lemma isClosed_indeterminacyLocus {X Y : Scheme.{u}} (f : X.RationalMap Y) :
140	    IsClosed (indeterminacyLocus f) := by
141	  unfold indeterminacyLocus
142	  exact (f.domain.isOpen).isClosed_compl
143	
144	/-! ## §2. Codimension-1-indeterminacy-free rational maps
145	
146	A rational map `f : X ⇢ Y` is **codim-1-indeterminacy-free** if its
147	indeterminacy locus contains no prime divisor of `X`, i.e. every codim-1
148	generic point of `X` already lies in `f.domain`. Equivalently (under the
149	project's standing `Order.coheight` convention on the specialisation
150	preorder of `X.carrier`), every `η : X` with `Order.coheight η = 1` lies in
151	`f.domain`.
152	
153	Blueprint pin: `def:codim_one_indeterminacy` (Milne §I.3 Theorem 3.1, the
154	conclusion). -/
155	
156	/-- **Codim-1-indeterminacy-free rational map.** A rational map
157	`f : X ⇢ Y` is codim-1-indeterminacy-free if every codimension-one point
158	of `X` lies in its domain of definition. Equivalently: no prime divisor of
159	`X` (i.e. closure of a codim-1 generic point) is contained in
160	`indeterminacyLocus f`.
161	
162	The codim-1 condition is encoded via `Order.coheight η = 1` on the
163	specialisation preorder of `X.carrier`, matching the project's standing
164	convention in `Scheme.PrimeDivisor` (`RiemannRoch/WeilDivisor.lean`).
165	
166	Blueprint reference: `def:codim_one_indeterminacy` (Milne, *Abelian
167	Varieties*, Theorem 3.1, §I.3, p. 16). -/
168	def CodimOneFree {X Y : Scheme.{u}} (f : X.RationalMap Y) : Prop :=
169	  ∀ (x : X), Order.coheight x = 1 → x ∈ f.domain
170	
171	end RationalMap

173	/-! ## §3. Smoothness yields a DVR at every codim-1 point
174	
175	On a nonsingular variety `X` over `k̄`, the local ring at the generic point
176	of a prime divisor is a discrete valuation ring with fraction field equal to
177	the function field `K(X) = X.functionField`. This is Hartshorne II.6's
178	"regular in codimension one" property combined with the regular-local-ring-of-dim-1
179	$\Leftrightarrow$ DVR equivalence (Atiyah–Macdonald 9.2; Stacks `00PD`).
180	
181	Blueprint pin: `lem:smooth_codim_one_dvr` (Hartshorne II.6 p. 130). -/
182	
183	/-- **Smooth + codim-1 ⇒ DVR.** For a nonsingular integral variety `X` over
184	an algebraically closed field `k̄` and a point `η : X.left` with
185	`Order.coheight η = 1`, the stalk `X.left.presheaf.stalk η` is a discrete
186	valuation ring (with fraction field the function field `K(X)`).
187	
188	The proof reduces to "regular local ring of Krull dimension `1` is a DVR"
189	(Mathlib `IsDiscreteValuationRing.isDiscreteValuationRing_iff` or analogous)
190	plus the smooth-variety fact that every local ring at a codim-1 point is
191	regular of dimension `1`.
192	
193	Blueprint reference: `lem:smooth_codim_one_dvr` (Hartshorne II.6 p. 130;
194	Stacks `00PD` for the regular-of-dim-1 ⇔ DVR equivalence). -/
195	theorem localRing_dvr_of_codim_one
196	    {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]
197	    {X : Over (Spec (.of kbar))}
198	    [Smooth X.hom] [GeometricallyIrreducible X.hom]
199	    [IsSeparated X.hom] [LocallyOfFiniteType X.hom]
200	    [IsIntegral X.left] [IsReduced X.left]
201	    (z : X.left) (_hz : Order.coheight z = 1) :
202	    IsDiscreteValuationRing (X.left.presheaf.stalk z) :=
203	  sorry

207	/-! ## §4. Milne Theorem 3.1 — codim-≥ 2 extension to a complete target
208	
209	A rational map from a nonsingular variety to a complete variety has
210	indeterminacy locus of codimension `≥ 2`. Specialising further: if `f` is
211	already codim-1-indeterminacy-free (by hypothesis), then `Dom(f) = X` and
212	`f` extends to a unique regular morphism `X ⟶ Y`.
213	
214	Blueprint pin: `thm:codim_one_extension` (Milne §I.3 Theorem 3.1 p. 16). -/
215	
216	/-- **Milne Theorem 3.1 (specialised) — codim-1-indeterminacy-free + smooth
217	source + complete target ⇒ extension.**
218	
219	For `X` a nonsingular variety over an algebraically closed field `k̄`,
220	`Y` a complete variety over `k̄`, and `f : X ⇢ Y` a codim-1-indeterminacy-free
221	rational map, there exists a *unique* regular morphism `g : X ⟶ Y`
222	whose induced rational map equals `f`.
223	
224	The proof combines two steps:
225	* **Step 1** (codim-1 ruling-out): the indeterminacy locus has no codim-1
226	  components, because at the generic point of any candidate prime divisor
227	  `W ⊆ Z(f)` the DVR property of the stalk (`localRing_dvr_of_codim_one`)
228	  feeds the valuative criterion of properness on the complete target `Y`.
229	* **Step 2** (codim-≥2 extension): at a remaining codim-≥2 point `x`, the
230	  depth-≥2 property of the regular local ring `O_{X,x}` (Cohen–Macaulay
231	  from `cor:regular_cohen_macaulay` of `Albanese/AuslanderBuchsbaum.lean`)
232	  forces `H¹_{x}(V, O_X) = 0` and the pulled-back coordinates extend
233	  uniquely. By the `CodimOneFree` hypothesis, Step 1 alone is already
234	  enough on the dim-≤2 cases the project consumes; Step 2 is what extends
235	  through the remaining higher-codim points to obtain `Dom(f) = X`.
236	
237	Uniqueness is the standard reduced-and-separated agreement principle
238	(`AlgebraicGeometry.ext_of_isDominant`): two morphisms `X ⟶ Y` that agree
239	on a dense open of the reduced scheme `X` agree everywhere.
240	
241	Blueprint reference: `thm:codim_one_extension` (Milne, *Abelian Varieties*,
242	Theorem 3.1, §I.3, p. 16). -/
243	theorem extend_of_codimOneFree_of_smooth
244	    {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]
245	    {X : Over (Spec (.of kbar))}
246	    [Smooth X.hom] [GeometricallyIrreducible X.hom]
247	    [IsSeparated X.hom] [LocallyOfFiniteType X.hom]
248	    [IsIntegral X.left] [IsReduced X.left]
249	    {Y : Over (Spec (.of kbar))}
250	    [IsProper Y.hom] [GeometricallyIrreducible Y.hom]
251	    [IsSeparated Y.hom] [LocallyOfFiniteType Y.hom]
252	    [IsIntegral Y.left] [IsReduced Y.left]
253	    (f : X.left.RationalMap Y.left) (_hf : CodimOneFree f) :
254	    ∃! (g : X.left ⟶ Y.left), g.toRationalMap = f :=
255	  sorry

257	/-! ## §5. Milne Lemma 3.3 — pure-codim-1 indeterminacy into a group variety
258	
259	A rational map from a nonsingular variety to a group variety has its
260	indeterminacy locus either empty or of pure codimension `1`. Combined with
261	`thm:codim_one_extension`'s codim-≥2 conclusion, this forces the locus to
262	be empty when the target is an abelian variety.
263	
264	Blueprint pin: `lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy` (Milne §I.3 Lemma 3.3 p. 17). -/
265	
266	/-- **Milne Lemma 3.3 — indeterminacy into a group variety is empty or pure
267	codim 1.**
268	
269	For `X` a nonsingular variety over `k̄`, `G` a group variety over `k̄`
270	(a smooth group scheme of finite type, separated), and `f : X ⇢ G` a
271	rational map, the indeterminacy locus `Z(f) := indeterminacyLocus f` is
272	either empty (`f` is defined on all of `X`) or every point of `Z(f)` lies in
273	the closure of a codim-1 generic point.
274	
275	The proof (Milne's): consider the difference map
276	`Φ : X × X ⇢ G`, `(x, y) ↦ f(x) · f(y)⁻¹`. Then `Φ` is defined at `(x, x)`
277	iff `f` is defined at `x`. Pulling back the maximal ideal of `O_{G,e}` gives
278	a finite set of rational functions on `X × X` whose pole divisors are pure
279	codim-1; their intersection with the diagonal exhibits the indeterminacy
280	locus of `f` as pure codim-1.
281	
282	Blueprint reference: `lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy` (Milne, *Abelian
283	Varieties*, Lemma 3.3, §I.3, p. 17). -/
284	theorem indeterminacy_pure_codim_one_into_grpScheme
285	    {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]
286	    {X : Over (Spec (.of kbar))}
287	    [Smooth X.hom] [GeometricallyIrreducible X.hom]
288	    [IsSeparated X.hom] [LocallyOfFiniteType X.hom]
289	    [IsIntegral X.left] [IsReduced X.left]
290	    {G : Over (Spec (.of kbar))}
291	    [GrpObj G] [Smooth G.hom] [GeometricallyIrreducible G.hom]
292	    [IsSeparated G.hom] [LocallyOfFiniteType G.hom]
293	    [IsIntegral G.left] [IsReduced G.left]
294	    (f : X.left.RationalMap G.left) :
295	    indeterminacyLocus f = ∅ ∨
296	      ∀ x ∈ indeterminacyLocus f,
297	        ∃ (z : X.left), Order.coheight z = 1 ∧ x ∈ closure ({z} : Set X.left) :=
298	  sorry

300	/-! ## §6. Weil-divisor reformulation of the codim-1 extension obstruction
301	
302	The codim-1 extension obstruction of `thm:codim_one_extension` has a clean
303	reformulation in the project's Weil-divisor language: `f` extends at the
304	generic point of a prime divisor `W ⊆ X` iff every regular function on an
305	affine neighbourhood of the image pulls back to a function of order `≥ 0`
306	along `W` (no pole at `W`).
307	
308	This is the form the consumer in
309	`Albanese/AlbaneseUP.lean` (A.4.d) needs when combining
310	`def:indeterminacy_locus`, `def:order_at_point`, and `def:principal_divisor`.
311	
312	Blueprint pin: `thm:weil_divisor_obstruction` (Hartshorne II.6 pp. 130–131). -/
313	
314	/-- **Weil-divisor characterisation of the codim-1 extension obstruction.**
315	
316	For `X` a nonsingular variety over `k̄`, `Y` a variety over `k̄`,
317	`f : X ⇢ Y` a rational map, and `W : X.left.PrimeDivisor` a prime divisor
318	of `X` (encoded by its generic point `W.point` with codim-1 witness
319	`Order.coheight W.point = 1`), the following are equivalent:
320	
321	1. `W.point ∈ f.domain` (i.e. `f` is defined at the generic point of `W`).
322	2. There exists a `PartialMap` representative `g` of `f` (a regular morphism
323	   `g.hom : g.domain ⟶ Y.left` from a dense open `g.domain ⊆ X.left` with
324	   `g.toRationalMap = f`) whose domain contains `W.point`.
325	
326	The equivalence (1) ⇔ (2) is the Hartshorne II.6 "regular = no pole"
327	criterion for a rational map at a DVR-stalk codim-1 point: by
328	`localRing_dvr_of_codim_one`, the stalk `O_{X,W.point}` is a DVR inside the
329	function field, and the standard valuation-ring characterisation
330	`O_{X,W.point} = {s ∈ K(X) | ord_W s ≥ 0} ∪ {0}` shows that the existence
331	of a representative defined at `W.point` is equivalent to all pullbacks
332	of regular functions on `Y` having order `≥ 0` at `W`. The Weil-divisor
333	identification feeds the existing `Scheme.RationalMap.order` API from
334	`RiemannRoch/WeilDivisor.lean`.
335	
336	Blueprint reference: `thm:weil_divisor_obstruction` (Hartshorne, *Algebraic
337	Geometry*, II.6 pp. 130–131; Stacks `02ME`).
338	
339	iter-178+ body: by `localRing_dvr_of_codim_one`, `O_{X,W.point}` is a DVR
340	inside the function field with valuation `Scheme.RationalMap.order W`; a
341	rational map is defined at `W.point` iff its pullback lands inside this
342	order-≥0 subring (the standard "regular = no pole" criterion). The forward
343	implication uses the canonical "partial map at W.point" extraction from
344	`Scheme.RationalMap.mem_domain`; the reverse implication invokes the
345	valuative criterion applied at the DVR `O_{X,W.point}`. -/
346	theorem extend_iff_order_nonneg
347	    {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]
348	    {X : Over (Spec (.of kbar))}
349	    [Smooth X.hom] [GeometricallyIrreducible X.hom]
350	    [IsSeparated X.hom] [LocallyOfFiniteType X.hom]
351	    [IsIntegral X.left] [IsReduced X.left] [IsLocallyNoetherian X.left]
352	    {Y : Over (Spec (.of kbar))}
353	    [IsSeparated Y.hom] [LocallyOfFiniteType Y.hom]
354	    [IsIntegral Y.left] [IsReduced Y.left]
355	    (f : X.left.RationalMap Y.left) (W : X.left.PrimeDivisor)
356	    [Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.left.presheaf.stalk W.point)] :
357	    W.point ∈ f.domain ↔
358	      ∃ (g : X.left.PartialMap Y.left),
359	        g.toRationalMap = f ∧ W.point ∈ (g.domain : Set X.left) :=
360	  sorry
361	
362	end RationalMap
363	
364	end Scheme

203	/-! ## §1. The Abel–Jacobi morphism at a marked point
204	
205	The first sub-lemma packages the construction and elementary regularity
206	of the Abel–Jacobi morphism `ι_{P₀} : C ⟶ Pic⁰_{C/k̄}` at the basepoint
207	`P₀ ∈ C(k̄)`. On `k̄`-points,
208	`ι_{P₀}(Q) = [𝓞_C(Q − P₀)] ∈ Pic⁰_{C/k̄}(k̄)`; at the basepoint
209	itself `ι_{P₀}(P₀) = η_J`.
210	
211	This is the content gated on A.3 (existence of `Pic⁰_{C/k̄}` as a
212	representable group scheme); the morphism is the moduli classifier of
213	the rigidified diagonal correspondence
214	`𝓛^{P₀} = 𝓞_{C × C}(Δ − {P₀} × C − C × {P₀})` on `C × C`, taken as a
215	relative degree-zero line bundle on `C × C` over the second factor.
216	
217	Blueprint reference: `lem:abel_jacobi_morphism` (Milne, *Abelian
218	Varieties*, Proposition III.6.1 and Summary III.6.11). -/
219	
220	/-- **The Abel–Jacobi morphism at the marked basepoint `P₀`.**
221	
222	For a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve `C` over an
223	algebraically closed field `k̄` and a marked `k̄`-point
224	`P₀ : 𝟙_ ⟶ C`, the **Abel–Jacobi morphism** is the unique regular
225	morphism `ι_{P₀} : C ⟶ Pic⁰_{C/k̄}` characterised on `T`-points by the
226	formula `(1 × ι_{P₀})^* 𝓜^{P₀} ≈ 𝓛^{P₀}`, where `𝓜^{P₀}` is the
227	Poincaré–Picard correspondence on `C × Pic⁰_{C/k̄}` and
228	`𝓛^{P₀} = 𝓞_{C × C}(Δ − {P₀} × C − C × {P₀})`.
229	
230	iter-178+: body constructed by applying the moduli interpretation of
231	`Pic⁰_{C/k̄}` (`def:pic_scheme` together with the identity-component
232	refinement A.3) to the relative degree-zero line bundle `𝓛^{P₀}` on
233	`C × C → C` along the second projection. Yoneda then produces the
234	unique classifying morphism `C ⟶ Pic⁰_{C/k̄}`. The basepoint
235	condition `P₀ ≫ abelJacobi C P₀ = η[Pic⁰_{C/k̄}]` (`lem:abel_jacobi_morphism`)
236	holds because restriction of `𝓛^{P₀}` to `C × {P₀}` is the trivial
237	line bundle `𝓞_C(P₀ − P₀) ≅ 𝓞_C` whose moduli class is `η_J`.
238
